segments.s028-g65: This relative clause begins with که introducing 'he who/that'. هر کاو functions as 'whoever / he who' (هر = any/whoever; کاو here is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'he who' rather than the lexical senses listed). نبید جوانی is a genitive-like phrase 'the wine (or intoxication) of youth' and چشید is a simple past verb 'tasted' or metaphorically 'experienced'.
segments.s028-g66: به گیتی is a prepositional phrase 'in/within the world' (به + گیتی). جز از خویشتن را ندید is the main assertion: جز = 'except', از = 'from/of', خویشتن را is the emphatic/definite self as object/topic, and ندید is the past negative 'saw not' — together: 'saw nothing in the world except himself.' Word order places the prepositional phrase and exception before the verb, as is normal in poetic/classical Persian.
segments.s028-g67: بدان مستی = 'by/with that drunkenness' (بدان is a fused demonstrative/particle + preposition). اندر دهد is an older/periphrastic verbal construction meaning 'causes' or 'brings about', and سر بباد is a fixed expression 'the head be blown away' = 'to lose one's head / be ruined.' Literally: 'That drunkenness causes the head to be blown away.'
segments.s028-g68: This is a jussive/wish clause with مباد (optative negative) at the end: 'تو را روز جز شاد و خرم مباد' = 'May there be no day for you except happy and joyful.' تو را marks the beneficiary/dative/topic ('for you'); روز is the subject ('a day'); جز شاد و خرم restricts it ('except joyful and glad'); and مباد negates/wishes negatively ('may it not be').
segments.s028-g33: نگهبان او is a possessive structure meaning 'his guard' (noun followed by the pronoun او). پای کرده is a participial phrase 'having set/placed his foot' (پای کردن = to plant one's foot), and به‌کش is a poetically contracted present/participial form from کشیدن meaning 'drawing/pulling' (here an action simultaneous with 'نشسته'). The three parts form a cluster of noun + participles describing the guard's posture and action before the main verb.
segments.s028-g34: نشسته is a past/participial form meaning 'sat' or 'seated'. به + بیشه درون gives a locative phrase 'in/at the grove within...', where به marks location and درون ('inside') intensifies it. شاه‌فش names the royal precinct/authority and completes the locative phrase. Word order places the participle before the locative adjunct, typical of classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s028-g35: بدو here is a dative/enclitic-like prepositional phrase meaning 'to him/unto him' (the indirect object precedes the verb). دادمت is past tense 'I gave you' with clitic object -ت (you). روزگاری دراز is an adverbial phrase 'for a long time' (روزگاری = time/period; دراز = long) modifying the giving.
segments.s028-g36: همی is an archaic/prosaic continuous/frequentive particle meaning 'continually/still'. پروردیدت is past tense 'he nurtured/reared you' with the 2nd-person object suffix -ت. به بر بر is a doubled locative phrase meaning 'upon the bosom/arms' (بر = on/upon; repetition is emphatic or idiomatic for 'in his arms/lap'). به ناز is an instrumental phrase 'with delicacy/affection/pampering' (ناز = fondness). The overall clause describes ongoing nurture done lovingly.
segments.s028-g37: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' marking source. پستان آن گاو is a genitive phrase 'the udder/breast of that cow' (پستان used here as udder). طاووس رنگ is an adjectival compound 'peacock‑colored' (طاووس 'peacock' modifying رنگ 'color') describing the thing produced. The phrase indicates origin and the coloration of what came from the cow.
segments.s028-g38: برافراختی is a 2nd-person singular past tense verb 'you raised/erected/produced' (the -ی ending marks 'you'). چون introduces a simile 'like/as', and دلاور پلنگ is the simile's noun phrase 'a valiant/leonine leopard' (دلاور qualifying پلنگ). Grammatically the verb states the action and the following phrase compares the result to a brave leopard.
segments.s028-g39: سرانجام is an adverb meaning 'finally' or 'in the end'. زآن (= ز + آن) is the source/prepositional 'of/from that', connecting to گاو و آن مرغزار ('that cow and that meadow') as the topic of the news. The whole phrase sets up what the following clause is about.
segments.s028-g40: یکایک is an adverb 'one by one' or 'each in turn'. خبر شد combines خبر ('news/report') with شد (here 'became/was [made]'), functioning idiomatically as 'the news came' or 'it was reported'. سوی شهریار is a prepositional phrase 'to/toward the king' (سوی = to/towards; شهریار = king), indicating the recipient of the reports.
segments.s028-g49: دلش (دل + possessive -ش) = 'his heart'; گشت is the past of گشتن used as 'became/turned'. پردرد is a predicative adjective 'full of pain/sorrow'. The clause then continues with و سر پر ز کین where سر 'head' takes the adjective phrase پر ز + noun: 'full (پر) of (ز) hatred (کین)'. Word order is Subject(possessed noun) + verb + predicate, and the suffix -ش marks possession.
segments.s028-g50: This is a compact, slightly inverted clause: به ابرو 'to the brow' (به as a directional/locative) + ز خشم 'from/through anger' (ز + noun) + اندر آورد چین 'he brought/caused a wrinkle within' (اندر آورد = 'brought/caused in/onto', چین = 'wrinkle'). In Persian the locative/goal phrase appears before the verb; آورد here has the sense 'cause/bring about' so the whole means 'he furrowed/knitted his brow in anger.'
segments.s028-g51: چنین 'thus' modifies the following verb clause. داد پاسخ 'gave an answer' is literal (داد = past of دادن, here 'gave'; پاسخ = 'answer') and به مادر marks the indirect object 'to his mother'. The conjunction که introduces the content clause that follows ('that/that a...').
segments.s028-g52: نگردد is the negative present/subjunctive/general-statement form of گشتن used here as 'does not become / is not made'. مگر = 'except' (introducing the condition). ز آزمایش دلیر = 'by/through the trial/ordeal of the brave' (ز = 'from/through', آزمایش here = 'ordeal/test', دلیر = 'brave'). The clause states a general truth: 'a lion does not come to be except by the testing of the brave.'
segments.s028-g53: کنون 'now' sets the time. کردنی is a noun/adjectival form here meaning 'that which must be done / the necessary act' (کردنی) and کرد is the past verb 'did' — the idiom کردنی کرد means 'he did what had to be done' or 'now he carried out the necessary action'. جادوپرست follows as the object/topic 'the sorcerer/magician.'
segments.s028-g54: This line uses an elliptical/archaic order. باید is the modal 'must/it is necessary'; it governs the verb برد which here functions as the needed action ('take/deal with') though the precise lexical sense is not given in the supplied sense list. مرا + را are the object/topic pieces (the segment list split them: م + را) marking the definite object; به شمشیر دست is an instrumental phrase 'by/with the sword-hand' (به + شمشیر + دست) meaning 'by my sword/with the sword in hand'. Overall: 'the sorcerer must be taken/handled by the sword (by my hand).'
segments.s028-g55: بپویم is a present/subjunctive/optative first-person form meaning 'I will go / I shall advance' (classical/poetic conjugation). به فرمان = 'by/at the command of' and یزدان پاک = 'the pure God' (یزدان 'God/divine power' modified by the adjective پاک). The prepositional phrase attaches to the verb: 'I will go at the command of the pure God.'
segments.s028-g56: برآرم is the first-person singular present/subjunctive of برآوردن meaning 'I will lift/raise' (here idiomatically 'raise dust' or 'cast down'); ز = 'from', ایوان ضحاک is a genitive phrase 'the iwan/porch of Zahhak', and خاک = 'dust/earth'. The phrase literally 'I will raise dust from Zahhak's iwan' meaning to overthrow or drive Zahhak out.
segments.s028-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a past-time clause; بگذشت is a past tense verb 'passed' (he/it passed by). از آن is a prepositional phrase 'from that' indicating the place/time just referred to; بر + فریدون is an appositive or directional phrase 'toward/to Fereydun' (here it attaches to the passing). The odd phrase دو هشت in the segment functions as an epithet or numeral attached to Fereydun (appositional) and is best treated as supplemental to the name rather than changing the clause's verb structure.
segments.s028-g2: ز (poetic form of از) introduces origin/place 'from'; البرز کوه is the noun phrase 'the Alborz mountain' (noun + noun, genitive-like sequence where'Alborz' specifies which mountain); اندر آمد is a compound past tense meaning 'came down/entered' (اندر + آمد = 'came into'), and به دشت is a directional prepositional phrase 'into the plain'. Word order is typical classical Persian: origin (ز البرز کوه) + motion verb (اندر آمد) + destination (به دشت).
segments.s028-g3: بر مادر آمد is a prepositional phrase + past verb meaning 'he came to (his) mother / approached his mother' (بر + object + آمد). پژوهید here is a past-tense verb 'inquired/sought' (classical usage meaning he asked or searched), and و گفت simply joins the clause with 'and said'. The sequence shows coordinated past actions: approach, inquire, then speak.
segments.s028-g4: که introduces a reported-subject clause 'that / (asking) that...'. بگشای is an imperative/subjunctive verb 'open/reveal' (ب- prefix + گشای) used as a request. بر من is the dative 'to me' (بر = to/toward here), نهان is the adjective/noun 'hidden/secret', از نهفت is an ablative/genitive-like phrase 'from what has been concealed' (نهفت = 'that which is hidden'). The clause is a direct request: 'that (you) reveal to me the thing hidden/kept secret.'
segments.s028-g5: بگو is an imperative 'say / tell'. مر is an older form of the dative/accusative 'to me' (functionally 'to me' or 'for me'); the following را is the object marker attached to whatever precedes (classical positioning here is irregular in the manuscript segmentation). تا که بودم is a past interrogative clause 'who was (my) father when I was?' where بودم is first-person past 'I was' and پدر is the noun 'father'. Overall this is a direct demand to be told the identity of his father, using past tense بودم in the embedded question.
segments.s028-g6: کیم من؟ is an inverted interrogative 'Who am I?' (کیم = who, followed by the pronoun من for emphasis). ز تخم introduces origin with 'from the seed/of the stock of' (ز = from); کدامین گهر is an interrogative noun phrase 'which jewel/gem' (کدامین = which, گهر = gem/essence), asking about his noble origin. The second sentence repeats the same question in genealogical terms.
segments.s028-g7: چه گویم is a rhetorical question 'what shall I say' where چه is the interrogative 'what' and گویم is a first‑person present/subjunctive form 'I should say / I may say' (classical Persian uses this form for deliberative questions). کیم is 'who am I' and بر سر انجمن is a locative idiom 'before/at the assembly' (بر سر = at/upon the head/face of; انجمن = assembly). Together it asks what one can say about one's identity in a public gathering.
segments.s028-g8: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain / one (tale)' used as 'some' or 'one of'. دانشی is a genitive/attributive form 'of knowledge / learned' modifying داستانم ('my story/tale'); داستانم is داستان + -م (possessive suffix) 'my tale'. بزن is an imperative 'strike / do it' used idiomatically here as 'tell/produce' (a common poetic usage meaning 'recite/relate'). The phrase requests that the mother (or speaker) relate a single instructive tale.
segments.s028-g57: "بدو گفت مادر که این رای نیست" is an instance of classical Persian word order where the indirect object clitic "بدو" ('to him') precedes the verb "گفت" (said). "مادر" is the speaker (postverbal subject), and "که" introduces reported speech (a subordinate clause). "این رای نیست" is the content of that reported speech: literally 'this counsel/opinion is not (so).' Note that "گفت" here is the simple past 'said' (poetic usage), and the clause is an indirect quotation introduced by "که."
segments.s028-g58: In "تو را با جهان سر به سر پای نیست" "تو را" is topical/dative-marked 'you' (the particle "را" gives emphasis/topic sense here), followed by the comitative phrase "با جهان" ('with the world'). "سر به سر" is an idiomatic sequence meaning 'head-to-head' or 'on equal terms' (each "سر" = 'head/top' with the linking "به"). The predicate "پای نیست" literally 'there is no foot' idiomatically means 'you have no standing/are no match.' The clause therefore asserts inability to contend with the world.
segments.s028-g59: This is a nominal apposition: "جهاندار ضحاک" = 'Zahhāk, the world-ruler' (title + name). The preposition "با" introduces attendant regalia/items "تاج و گاه" ('crown and gāh'); here "گاه" is a poetic word for a seat/occasion/place (listed sense 'time' is the closest), so the whole segment lists his trappings. The coordination "تاج و گاه" is simply linked by "و."
segments.s028-g60: This segment is a compact, somewhat inverted description. "میان بسته" is a participial phrase ('bound/placed in the midst' or 'having shut/closed between') modifying what follows; Persian poetry often omits an explicit copula/auxiliary. "فرمان او را" ('his command' with the object/possessive marked by "را") appears before the noun "سپاه" (army) for emphasis — so the army is linked to his command. The whole means that, in the middle/center, his army is set/held by his command (i.e. the army obeys/marches at his order).
segments.s028-g61: "چو خواهد" is a common poetic construction meaning 'when/whenever he wills' ("چو" = 'when', "خواهد" = 3rd-person singular present/future 'wants/wills'). "ز هر کشوری" means 'from every country/land' ("ز" = poetic 'from'), and "صدهزار" is the numeral 'one hundred thousand' used as the force of troops here. The sequence lists source and number of troops that can be raised whenever he desires.
segments.s028-g62: "کمر بسته" is a participial adjective phrase 'girded at the waist' (i.e. armed/prepared for battle). "او را" marks the target/recipient 'him' (object/topic-marking "را"). The verb "کند" (3rd sg. present/habitual 'does/makes') governs the action, and "کارزار" ('battle/war') is the object: together "او را کند کارزار" = 'makes war upon him.' The line therefore uses the participial qualifier before the verb and places the object with "را" before the verb — a common poetic inversion.
segments.s028-g63: "جز این است" literally 'except this is' functions idiomatically as 'this is nothing but / apart from this it is.' "آیین پیوند و کین" is a nominal phrase: "آیین" = 'custom/practice/ritual' followed by the coordinated nouns "پیوند و کین" ('bond/alliance and hatred/vengeance'), so the clause states that the rule/nature of things is a mixture of alliance and enmity. The copula "است" links the pronoun to the predicate.
segments.s028-g64: In "جهان را به چشم جوانی مبین" the noun phrase "جهان را" ('the world' with topical/object marker) is followed by the adverbial/agent phrase "به چشم جوانی" ('with the eye of youth' / 'through youthful eyes'). "مبین" here is an imperative/imperatival poetic form meaning 'see' or 'behold' (classical usage differs from the adjective sense listed in the lexicon, so the lexical senses given do not capture the imperative function). The whole is an exhortation: 'look at the world through the eyes of youth.'
segments.s028-g41: ز بیشه ببردم تو را ناگهان is literal word order 'from grove I-took-you suddenly': ز (= 'from') marks origin; بیشه is the place; ببردم is 1st-person past 'I carried/took away' used before the object; تو را uses the object pronoun with را (definite object marker) placed after the pronoun; ناگهان ('suddenly') is an adverb placed after the verb phrase for emphasis.
segments.s028-g42: گریزنده ز ایوان و از خان و مان is a participial phrase modifying the action/actor: گریزنده is the present participle 'fleeing'; two prepositional sources follow (ز ایوان and از خان) joined by و; را does not reappear here so this phrase is circumstantial ('while/when fleeing from...').
segments.s028-g43: بیامد بکشت آن گرانمایه را combines two verbal elements: بیامد 'he came' and بکشت (archaic past) 'he slew/put to death' — here the sequence reads as two successive actions. آن گرانمایه را is the direct object ('that precious one') marked by را.
segments.s028-g44: چنان بی‌زبان مهربان دایه را is a second object phrase in apposition or as an additional object: چنان 'such/so' intensifies the following adjectives; بی‌زبان 'mute' and مهربان 'kind' are attributive adjectives before the noun دایه 'nurse'; را again marks this noun as (another) definite object of the implied verb (بکشت).
segments.s028-g45: وز ایوان ما تا به خورشید خاک is a spatial/extent phrase: وز (a poetic variant related to 'از' or a gust-from) introduces the source 'ایوان ما' ('our iwan/veranda'); تا به خورشید = 'as far as/to the sun' gives the upward extent; خاک ('dust') is the thing being raised and so functions as the direct object of the verb that follows.
segments.s028-g46: برآورد و کرد آن بلندی مغاک contains two coordinated verbs: برآورد ('he brought up/raised' or 'produced') and کرد ('he made/did'), where the second verb states the result. آن بلندی = 'that height/greatness' is the theme, and مغاک ('abyss') is the result/complement: 'made that height into an abyss.' The coordination (verb و verb) is a common epic device.
segments.s028-g47: فریدون چو بشنید بگشاد گوش is a temporal clause plus main action: چو بشنید 'when he heard' (چو = 'when'); بگشاد گوش literally 'he opened his ear' — a stock idiom meaning 'he listened attentively' or 'he pricked up his ears.' Word order places the temporal clause before the resulting action.
segments.s028-g48: ز گفتار مادر برآمد به جوش is a causal/result phrase: ز گفتار مادر = 'from/because of his mother's speech' (ز = 'because/of/from'); برآمد 'arose' is the intransitive result; به جوش ('into boiling') is an idiomatic adverbial phrase meaning 'in a fury' or 'with great passion.'
segments.s028-g25: This short clause strings several nouns and modifiers before the verb in the next segment. کتف ضحاک is a possessive phrase ('the shoulder of Zahhak') with the ezāfe omitted as is common in epic poetry; ابر precedes and functions as a poetic epithet/modifier of کتف. جادو (magic) and دو مار (two snakes) are presented as agent and patient/theme respectively, but no finite verb appears here — the verbal action is supplied by the following segment.
segments.s028-g26: برست is a past-tense poetic verb (here carrying the sense ‘sprang up / came forth’ or related archaic senses) that completes the action introduced previously; the conjunction و links it to the following verb. برآورد is used causatively/predicatively ('brought about / wrought'); از ایران دمار is a prepositional phrase marking the affected realm ('ruin for / upon Iran') — in poetry از is used idiomatically to indicate the source/target of the calamity. Word order (verb after its noun-phrases) and the use of compressed, archaic verbal forms are typical of Ferdowsi’s style.
segments.s028-g27: سر is the direct object 'head'; بابت is an adverbial meaning 'as payment / in exchange'; از مغز literally 'from the brain' marks the material/source; پرداختند is the 3rd-person plural past of پرداختن used here in the sense 'they paid / they gave' (i.e. they satisfied a requirement or took/used something). The clause describes an action in compressed poetic diction where the precise idiom ('paid with the brain') is terse and idiomatic.
segments.s028-g28: همان is a demonstrative 'that very / the same'; اژدها را is the definite object (را marks the object explicitly); خورش ساختند is a compound where خورش ('stew / cooked dish') is the result and ساختند ('they made') is the verb 'they made into a stew.' In other words, the clause states that the dragon was turned into a dish — typical direct object + verb order with را highlighting definiteness.
segments.s028-g29: سرانجام is an adverb 'finally / at last'; رفتم is first-person singular past 'I went'; سوی is the preposition 'toward(s)'; بیشه‌ای is an indefinite noun 'a grove' (the -ای ending here makes it non-definite or 'a certain grove'). The clause follows the common Persian order where the adverb precedes the verb and the prepositional phrase marks direction.
segments.s028-g30: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause ('which / that'); نه کس (the negator نه before کس) means 'no one' (a classical/poetic negative phrasing); را is the object/topic marker used here for emphasis; زآن بیشه is 'of that grove' (زآن = 'from/of that'); اندیشه‌ای is a noun 'thought' used predicatively — the clause literally says 'that no one had thought of that grove,' with the full verbal paraphrase (e.g. 'had') implied but omitted, a common poetic ellipsis.
segments.s028-g31: یکی is an indefinite determiner 'a certain / one'; گاو is the noun 'cow'; دیدم is 'I saw' (1st-person past). چو introduces a simile 'like/as'; خرم is an adjective 'cheerful / lush'; بهار is the noun 'spring' (here the image of spring is used in the simile). Note that Persian poetry often omits the object marker را, so گاو precedes دیدم without را.
segments.s028-g32: سراپای (sara-pā) is a prefixed/adverbial form meaning 'head to toe / entirely' and here modifies the implied subject (the cow). نیرنگ و رنگ و نگار is a coordinated noun list ('trickery / artifice, color, and ornament'), linked by و; the whole phrase describes the cow's appearance as richly adorned and cunning, with the copula omitted (typical in verse).
segments.s028-g9: فرانک بدو گفت is an SOV clause: subject (فرانک) + indirect object (بدو, ‘to him’) + verb (گفت, ‘said’). کای is an archaic vocative marker ‘O’ addressing the next word (نامجوی), so کای نامجوی = “O Namjuy!” — the speaker is calling the addressee by name.
segments.s028-g10: بگویم تو را هر چه گفتی بگوی is reported speech following the vocative. بگویم here functions as a first‑person verbal form meaning roughly “I will tell” or “I tell” (older/poetic use of present/subjunctive instead of a modern future). تو را is the object with the emphatic/definite marker را attached to the pronoun ‘you’. هر چه together means ‘whatever’ (هر = any/whichever + چه = whatever), گفتی is ‘you have said/you said’, and بگوی is a hortative/imperfective form meaning ‘say it’ or ‘speak’ (an instruction to repeat or state what was said).
segments.s028-g11: تو بشناس is an imperative/hortative address: تو (you) + بشناس (be acquainted with/know). کز is the poetic contracted form of از که/که + از meaning ‘that/from which’; here it introduces the origin phrase ‘from the border of Iran-land’ (کز مرز ایران‌زمین = ‘that/of the borders of Iran’). The word order is poetic (verb after object phrase).
segments.s028-g12: یکی مرد بد نام او آبتین is a compact nominative statement with the copula omitted (typical in poetry). یکی (a certain) qualifies مرد (man); بد نام is a compound adjective meaning ‘of bad name’ = ‘ill‑reputed’ (نام here means reputation). او آبتین identifies him appositively (‘he [was] Abtin’) with the copula understood rather than stated.
segments.s028-g13: ز تخم کیان بود is a poetic origin clause: ز (from) + تخم (seed, i.e. stock/descendancy) + کیان (the Kian kings/royal house) + بود (was). The following و بیدار بود repeats the copula for rhythm: بیدار (awake/alert) + بود (was).
segments.s028-g14: خردمند و گرد و بی‌آزار بود lists predicates linked by و (and) with a final copula بود. خردمند = ‘wise’; گرد here is an adjectival/poetic word implying energetic or ‘going about’ (active, bustling) rather than the literal ‘around’ or ‘dust’ sense; بی‌آزار = ‘harmless’ or inoffensive. The final بود makes the whole series a past‑tense description.
segments.s028-g15: ز طهمورث گرد بودش نژاد is a slightly inverted poetic clause meaning ‘his stock/lineage was from Tahmuras’. ز (from) + طهمورث (Tahmuras) names the ancestor, نژاد (lineage/stock) is the noun, and بودش is بود with the enclitic -ش (= ‘it was’/‘his was’) functioning as the copula. گرد is a brief poetic modifier/adjective here tied into the predicate (a conventional poetic particle in older Persian to add cadence).
segments.s028-g16: پدر بر پدر بر همی داشت یاد uses repetition and particles to say ‘he kept remembrance of father upon father’ (i.e., he remembered his ancestors generation after generation). پدر بر پدر بر = ‘father upon father’ (بر = on/upon, here indicating succession), همی is a continuous/habitual marker (‘continuously’), داشت is the past tense ‘had/kept’, and یاد = ‘memory/remembrance’ (the object).
segments.s028-g17: This clause expresses an optative/wish. Word order is poetic: پدر (father) + بد (bad/evil) modifies it; تو را and مرا use the accusative/topic marker را to mark the persons affected (literary way to say “your …” and “my …” or “for you / for me”); شوی is the subjunctive/optative form of شدن (here “may become / may be”), so the whole clause means “may your father be bad and mine be good.”
segments.s028-g18: نبد is an archaic negative past (≈ نبـود, “was not”). روز روشن (‘bright/clear day’) is the nominal predicate; مرا here marks experiencer/beneficiary (“for me”). جز introduces an exception (“except”), and بدوی is an adjective meaning ‘original/primordial’. Together: “There was no bright day for me except the original one.”
segments.s028-g19: چنان بد که is a correlative clause head meaning “so/such (bad) that …”; که introduces the result clause. ضحاک is the proper name Zahhāk and جادوپرست (‘magic‑worshipper/sorcerer’) apposes/describes him.
segments.s028-g20: This is the result clause introduced by که: از ایران “from Iran,” به جان تو literally “to/for your life” (meaning “against your life / to take your life”), یازید is a poetic past/preterite meaning “gaped/opened/broke forth” (here idiomatically ‘reached out’), and دست = “hand.” The phrase دست یازیدن means ‘to reach out (one’s hand)’; so the clause reads “(he) from Iran reached out his hand toward your life.”
segments.s028-g21: از او ‘from him’ is the source; من is the first‑person subject; نهانت is an object/adjective meaning ‘hidden’ (you hidden); همی داشتم is a literary imperfect/continuous past (‘I used to keep / I kept’) with همی marking continuous aspect. Altogether: “I kept you hidden from him.”
segments.s028-g22: This is a rhetorical question: چه مایه = ‘what means / what resource’ (i.e. ‘what could I’), به بد روز = ‘into a bad day’ (into misfortune), and بگذاشتم is past of گذاشتن (‘I would put / I placed’). The sense: “What means had I to put/expose you to misfortune?”
segments.s028-g23: پدرت is ‘your father’ (پدر + 2nd‑person suffix ت). آن گرانمایه مرد جوان is a descriptive apposition: آن = ‘that,’ گرانمایه = ‘precious/esteemed,’ مرد جوان = ‘young man’ (mرد + جوان). The whole noun phrase = “Your father, that precious young man.”
segments.s028-g24: فدی کرده is a conventional literary expression meaning ‘has offered himself / has been devoted/sacrificed’; کرده is the perfect participle/auxiliary ‘has done.’ پیش تو = ‘before you / in your presence’ and روشن‑روان is an honorific compound ‘bright‑souled’ describing the addressee. Together: “(He) has sacrificed himself before you, bright‑souled one.”

sentences.s028-s33: He who has tasted the wine of youth sees nothing in the world except himself.
sentences.s028-s34: That intoxication will make you lose your head; may you have no day except merry and glad.
sentences.s028-s17: His guard, having planted his foot, sat drawing (his bow) in the grove within the royal precinct.
sentences.s028-s18: I gave you to him for a long while; he continually raised you in his arms with fond indulgence.
sentences.s028-s19: From that cow's udder you reared (it) peacock‑colored, like a valiant leopard.
sentences.s028-s20: At last the news of that cow and that meadow, one by one, reached the king.
sentences.s028-s25: His heart became full of sorrow and his head filled with hatred; in anger he drew a wrinkle to his brow.
sentences.s028-s26: He answered his mother thus: a lion is not made except by a trial of the brave.
sentences.s028-s27: Now he put the thing to be done into action: the sorcerer must be dealt with by the sword (by my hand).
sentences.s028-s28: I will go at the command of the pure God and raise dust from Zahhak's iwan (i.e. overthrow Zahhak).
sentences.s028-s1: When he passed that place, Fereydun — (the epithet) دو هشت — came down from the Alborz mountain into the plain.
sentences.s028-s2: He went to his mother, inquired, and said: (that) reveal to me what has been hidden.
sentences.s028-s3: Tell me: who was my father? Who am I? From the seed of which jewel (do I come)?
sentences.s028-s4: What shall I say, who am I before the assembly? Tell (me) one tale of wisdom.
sentences.s028-s29: His mother said to him, "This counsel is not right; you are no match for the world (to stand head-to-head)."
sentences.s028-s30: Zahhāk, lord of the world, with crown and throne, has marshaled an army at his command in the midst.
sentences.s028-s31: Whenever he wishes, he makes war on him, sending a hundred thousand (men) from every country, girded for battle.
sentences.s028-s32: This is nothing but the custom of alliance and enmity; behold the world with the eye of youth.
sentences.s028-s21: I suddenly carried you off from the grove, fleeing from the iwan and from the khan and the remnant.
sentences.s028-s22: He came and killed that precious one — likewise the kind, mute nurse.
sentences.s028-s23: From our iwan up to the sun he raised dust, and made that great height an abyss.
sentences.s028-s24: When Fereydun heard, he pricked up his ear; at his mother's words he rose in a fury.
sentences.s028-s13: On Zahhak’s shoulder magic produced two snakes, and it brought ruin upon Iran.
sentences.s028-s14: They cut off the heads, took the brains as payment, and made that very dragon into a stew.
sentences.s028-s15: At last I went toward a grove that no one had thought about.
sentences.s028-s16: I saw a certain cow, joyful as spring, its whole body full of trickery, color, and ornament.
sentences.s028-s5: Frank said to him, “O Namjuy, tell me whatever you have said.”
sentences.s028-s6: Know that from the border of the land of Iran there was a certain man of ill repute named Abtin.
sentences.s028-s7: He was of the seed of the Kians and was wakeful; he was wise, active, and harmless.
sentences.s028-s8: His lineage was from Tahmuras; he kept the memory of father upon father.
sentences.s028-s9: May your father be evil and mine good; there was no bright day for me except the original one.
sentences.s028-s10: So evil was he that Zahhāk the sorcerer stretched out his hand from Iran toward your life.
sentences.s028-s11: I kept you hidden from him; what means had I to put you into a bad day?
sentences.s028-s12: Your father, that precious young man, has offered himself before you, O bright‑souled one.
